title: Aspidium ellipticum
intro: Aspidium ellipticum, also known as the elliptical or tailed fern, is a species of fern that belongs to the family Dryopteridaceae. The plant is prized for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antipyretic effects, making it useful in treating conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Aspidium ellipticum include sesquiterpenoids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often used to make teas, infusions, and decoctions, which are commonly used to treat various health conditions, although more research is needed to confirm its efficacy and safety.
taxonomy: Aspidium ellipticum is classified within the genus Aspidium, which is now considered a synonym of the genus Woodsia in the family Woodsiaceae. The family Woodsiaceae is a part of the order Polypodiales, which is a group of ferns. The order Polypodiales is within the class Polypodiopsida, which is a subclass of the class Polypodiophyta. The class Polypodiophyta is a division of plants known as the Pteridophyta, or vascular cryptogams. Overall, Aspidium ellipticum's classification reflects its evolutionary relationships with other ferns.
